briefing.incidentSummary:
Riya Desai, a senior product manager at CloudMatrix, clicked a phishing link 6 weeks ago. Nothing seemed wrong — no password change, no alerts, no unusual activity.

Then a client reported that confidential project roadmaps had been downloaded and shared externally. All logs pointed to Riya's account.

Riya denied it. She was in a client meeting at the time, with 6 witnesses.

Authentication logs then revealed the impossible: her account was simultaneously active from both Mumbai and Frankfurt — within a 4-minute window.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Prove simultaneous sessions from two different countries

› Decode the JWT token to link the attacker session to the phishing event 6 weeks ago

› Compare browser fingerprints to confirm a different physical device

› Trace the Frankfurt IP to a known dark web broker

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: SilverKey

digitalFootprint.ipActivity: 45.77.190.22 — Frankfurt VPS — 6 weeks of session refreshes

suspects.profileNotes: The Frankfurt IP 45.77.190.22 is listed on dark web credential marketplaces — used by threat actor SilverKey for 14 prior session token sales. The phishing domain sso-cloudmatrix.net was registered 7 weeks ago and has hosted phishing pages for 6 other SaaS companies. SilverKey specialises in OAuth token theft at scale: steal the refresh token at the SSO redirect step, sell persistent account access on the dark web. The access to Riya's account was listed for $800. Three bids were received.
suspects.description: SilverKey is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Riya Desai
suspects.profileNotes: Account owner and victim. Was in a client meeting during the download event — confirmed by 6 witnesses. Simultaneous session data proves her account was accessed by a separate individual at the same time. Fell victim to a convincing SSO phishing page 6 weeks ago. Cleared of involvement.
suspects.description: Riya Desai is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Nikhil Sharma
suspects.profileNotes: Responsible for the SSO configuration that left OAuth refresh tokens non-expiring. This misconfiguration is what allowed SilverKey to maintain persistent access for 6 weeks without triggering a re-authentication prompt. Likely negligent rather than complicit — but the configuration failure directly enabled the attack. Browser fingerprint evidence rules out his device being the attacker session.
suspects.description: Nikhil Sharma is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Unknown Buyer (Competitor)

digitalFootprint.ipActivity: 45.77.190.22 — Same VPS as SilverKey — buyer routed through broker infrastructure
digitalFootprint.deviceUsage: Unknown — 1366×768 screen, Intel HD 620, 4 cores, 4 GB RAM

suspects.profileNotes: Paid $800 for access to Riya's account on a dark web marketplace. Blockchain records show the winning bid came from a wallet previously linked to an industrial espionage group targeting Series B and C tech startups. Has been conducting silent reconnaissance for 6 weeks — downloading roadmaps, monitoring project pipelines. Identity not fully established.
suspects.description: Unknown Buyer (Competitor) is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

items.label: Simultaneous Sessions — Mumbai & Frankfurt — 4-Minute Overlap
items.detail: SSO authentication logs for Riya Desai's account over the past 8 weeks reveal a sustained pattern of dual-location activity. During the download event: Riya's legitimate session shows active use from IP 103.21.44.88 (Mumbai, India) at 14:32:05. At 14:30:17 — just 108 seconds earlier — IP 45.77.190.22 (Frankfurt, Germany) initiated the bulk download of confidential project roadmaps. Both sessions were active and authenticated simultaneously for a 4-minute window. Mumbai to Frankfurt is a minimum 8-hour journey. This overlap is physically impossible for a single person. Session hijacking is confirmed.

items.label: Frankfurt IP — 6 Weeks of Silent Token Refreshes
items.detail: IP 45.77.190.22 (Frankfurt) has been silently calling the OAuth token refresh endpoint twice per day, every day, for 6 weeks — starting the same day as the phishing redirect. The attacker never needed to log in again after the initial token theft. Each refresh extends the session for another 12 hours. CloudMatrix's SSO configuration has refresh token rotation disabled, meaning the same token can be reused indefinitely without generating a new authentication event. The sustained 6-week access pattern is only possible because the token was never revoked.

rows.value: 6 weeks ago — same day as phishing attack
Persistent silent access — no re-auth triggered

minigame.title: Concurrent Session Detector
minigame.hint: Cross-reference login timestamps and geolocation. Find the window where two sessions from different countries are active at the same time.
minigame.instruction: Review the session timeline below. Drag the two overlapping session markers to identify the 4-minute concurrent window. Flag the Frankfurt IP as the attacker session and confirm the geographic impossibility.
hints: Look for two sessions that are both active at the same time. Find the time window where both IP addresses show authenticated activity.
The Frankfurt session starts at 14:30:17. Riya's Mumbai session starts at 14:28:00. Both are active between 14:30:17 and 14:34:29 — a 4-minute overlap.
Mumbai to Frankfurt is 7,200 km — at least 8 hours by plane. Two simultaneous sessions from both locations is physically impossible. Flag the Frankfurt IP as the attacker.

onSuccess.message: SESSION HIJACKING CONFIRMED — Two simultaneous sessions from Mumbai and Frankfurt. Physically impossible for one person. Attacker has been using Riya's account. Session Token Records now unlocked.

items.label: JWT Refresh Token — Issued 6 Weeks Ago — Never Revoked
items.detail: The JWT (JSON Web Token) session token associated with the Frankfurt IP sessions has been decoded. The sub field confirms the token was legitimately issued to riya.desai@cloudmatrix.io. The iat (issued-at) timestamp is 1706745600 — converting to human-readable format gives the exact date of the phishing attack 6 weeks ago. The exp field reads 'Never' — CloudMatrix's SSO configuration does not expire refresh tokens. The jti (token ID) f8a3c2d1-9b7e-44f0 matches the token logged in the SSO redirect event at the Frankfurt IP on the phishing day. The token was stolen at the redirect step — when Riya's browser was sent to the fake SSO page — and has been reused 84 times since without triggering any alert.

rows.value: Matches Frankfurt phishing redirect event log

minigame.title: JWT Token Decoder
minigame.hint: Decode the Base64-encoded JWT token. Extract the iat field and convert the Unix timestamp to confirm when this token was originally issued.
minigame.instruction: The JWT below is encoded in three Base64 parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature. Decode the payload section, extract the iat Unix timestamp, and convert it to a date. Then match the jti field against the phishing redirect log.
hints: A JWT has three sections separated by dots. The middle section (payload) is Base64-encoded. Decode it to see the token's claims — sub, iat, exp, and jti.
The iat field is a Unix timestamp: 1706745600. Converting this gives the date 6 weeks ago — exactly when the phishing attack occurred.
Match the jti value f8a3c2d1-9b7e-44f0 against the phishing redirect log. Same token ID = same token. It was stolen 6 weeks ago and never revoked.

onSuccess.message: TOKEN THEFT CONFIRMED — JWT was issued on the phishing day and has never expired. The attacker has held persistent access for 6 weeks using a single stolen refresh token. Browser Fingerprints panel now unlocked.

items.label: Browser Fingerprint — 11 of 14 Fields Differ — Different Physical Device
items.detail: Side-by-side comparison of browser fingerprint data between Riya's legitimate Mumbai sessions and the Frankfurt attacker sessions shows 11 out of 14 fields are different. Critically, the differences extend beyond software settings (which a VPN could explain) into hardware-level characteristics: canvas hash, GPU renderer (NVIDIA GTX 1650 vs Intel HD 620), CPU cores (8 vs 4), device memory (16 GB vs 4 GB), and screen resolution (1920×1080 vs 1366×768) all differ. A VPN changes IP address and timezone — it cannot change hardware. These 11 mismatches confirm the attacker session comes from a completely different physical machine, not from Riya using a VPN or different browser profile.

rows.value: Riya: NVIDIA GTX 1650 | Attacker: Intel HD 620
Different physical device — VPN explanation ruled out

minigame.title: Browser Fingerprint Comparator
minigame.hint: Compare the two session fingerprints side by side. Count the number of non-matching fields and determine whether the differences are consistent with VPN use or a genuinely different physical device.
minigame.instruction: Review each fingerprint field for Riya's session (Mumbai) vs the attacker session (Frankfurt). Mark each field as MATCH or MISMATCH. Then determine: can a VPN explain all the differences?
hints: Go through each row and mark MATCH or MISMATCH. Count how many fields differ between the two sessions.
After counting mismatches, ask: which differences could a VPN explain? A VPN changes IP, timezone, and language — but it cannot change GPU model, CPU cores, or RAM.

11 of 14 fields differ. Hardware indicators like WebGL renderer (NVIDIA vs Intel), CPU cores (8 vs 4), and device memory (16 GB vs 4 GB) confirm this is a completely different physical machine — not Riya on a VPN.

onSuccess.message: DIFFERENT DEVICE CONFIRMED — 11 of 14 fingerprint fields differ. Hardware mismatches rule out VPN. The attacker is operating from a separate physical machine. Dark Web Intelligence panel now unlocked.

items.label: IP 45.77.190.22 — Dark Web Credential Marketplace — SilverKey
items.detail: Threat intelligence platform lookup on IP 45.77.190.22 returns a direct hit: the address is listed on a dark web credential marketplace as infrastructure used by broker 'SilverKey' for 14 prior session token sales. SilverKey is a known initial access broker specialising in OAuth token theft and resale, active since 2022. The Frankfurt IP is not a random VPS — it is dedicated infrastructure for a professional token resale operation.

rows.value: Listed on dark web credential marketplace

items.label: sso-cloudmatrix.net — Phishing Domain — 6 Other Targets
items.detail: The phishing domain sso-cloudmatrix.net was registered 7 weeks ago — one week before the attack on Riya. Hosting history analysis shows the same server has hosted phishing pages for 6 other SaaS companies. This is a mass-harvest operation, not a targeted attack against CloudMatrix specifically. Riya was one of many victims across multiple platforms. The listing on the dark web marketplace read: 'Senior product manager at a SaaS company, full session access — $800.' It received 3 bids.

rows.value: 6 other SaaS companies on same server
3 — winning bid from industrial espionage wallet

items.label: Correlation Matrix Brief
items.detail: Cross-case behaviour fragments were extracted from logs, commit metadata, and communication fingerprints. Match each fragment to the suspect it most strongly identifies.

hiddenItem.label: Hidden Suspect Unmasked
hiddenItem.detail: The matrix reveals a previously obscured operator profile tied to SilverKey. Their unique workflow signature and language fingerprints align across all critical events.

minigame.title: Suspect Correlation Matrix
minigame.instruction: Match each forensic fragment to the most likely suspect. Only one mapping set is fully consistent.

fragments.label: Workflow Fingerprint
fragments.value: Task execution cadence matches an operator who batches actions in 17-minute windows and pivots immediately after privileged checks.
fragments.hint: Compare this to known operating patterns.
fragments.explanation: This disciplined pattern matches SilverKey, who used the same cadence during the breach stages.

fragments.label: Noise Signature
fragments.value: An intentionally noisy decoy pattern appears in low-value logs with inconsistent timezone usage.
fragments.hint: This fragment points to a distractor suspect.
fragments.explanation: The inconsistent decoy style maps to Riya Desai and does not align with the core intrusion chain.

fragments.label: Language Marker
fragments.value: Incident notes reuse a rare phrase sequence that appears in insider coordination messages.
fragments.hint: Look for the suspect with matching communication style.
fragments.explanation: The phrase reuse is linked to Nikhil Sharma, but it appears only in support communications, not execution logs.

onSuccess.message: Hidden suspect profile unlocked: SilverKey emerges as the only suspect matching the complete matrix.

items.label: Breach Window Interrogation Notes
items.detail: Interview transcripts captured each suspect's alibi during the critical breach window. Verify which alibi collapses against timeline telemetry.

hiddenItem.label: Critical Alibi Contradiction
hiddenItem.detail: Timeline reconstruction disproves SilverKey's alibi. Their claimed location conflicts with system access records at the breach timestamp.

minigame.title: Breach Window Alibi Check
minigame.instruction: Review each alibi and identify the one contradicted by forensic timeline data.

timelineEvent.event: Privilege escalation and staged exfiltration occurred during this exact interval.

alibis.alibi: Claims to be offline in a routine maintenance meeting with no system access.
alibis.contradiction: Authentication and command logs show privileged actions from this suspect's account during the same window.

alibis.alibi: States they were in a monitored support call with screen recording enabled.
Reports compiling a non-production patch with signed CI traces.

onSuccess.message: Contradiction confirmed: SilverKey's alibi collapses under timeline verification.

timeline.title: Phishing Domain Registered
timeline.description: sso-cloudmatrix.net registered by SilverKey. Phishing page mirrors CloudMatrix's real SSO login.

timeline.title: Phishing Email Sent to Riya
timeline.description: Riya receives convincing email from 'CloudMatrix SSO'. Clicks link and enters credentials on fake page.

timeline.title: OAuth Token Stolen at SSO Redirect
timeline.description: Phishing page captures Riya's OAuth refresh token at the redirect step. Token issued to Frankfurt IP 45.77.190.22.

timeline.title: Session Access Listed for $800
timeline.description: SilverKey lists access to Riya's account on dark web marketplace. 3 bids. Industrial espionage group wins.

timeline.title: Silent Reconnaissance — 84 Token Refreshes
timeline.description: Buyer silently accesses account twice daily. Reads project roadmaps, monitors client workspaces. No alerts triggered.

timeline.title: Bulk Download — Confidential Roadmaps
timeline.description: Frankfurt session bulk-downloads confidential project roadmaps from a client workspace.

timeline.title: Riya Active in Client Meeting
timeline.description: Riya's legitimate Mumbai session active simultaneously. 6 witnesses confirm her presence. Impossible overlap detected.

timeline.title: Security Team Pulls Auth Logs
timeline.description: Simultaneous sessions from two countries identified. Session hijacking investigation begins.

updates.note: Simultaneous sessions clear Riya immediately. External actor confirmed. Frankfurt IP rises sharply.
Token linked to phishing event. SSO misconfiguration puts Nikhil under scrutiny. Broker pattern confirmed.
Different hardware device rules out Nikhil using same machine. External broker and buyer both rise to Critical.
SilverKey confirmed as the IP owner. 14 prior token sales. The buyer's identity remains partially unresolved.

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Session hijacking via OAuth token theft confirmed. SilverKey identified as the initial access broker. The buyer's identity remains a thread for further investigation.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct actor identified but the token forensics chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: Riya was a victim, not the perpetrator. The attacker was an external broker who stole her session token through a phishing page and sold persistent access for $800.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

coldCase.title: Case Gone Cold
coldCase.subtitle: The session token was never revoked. SilverKey keeps refreshing it every 12 hours.
coldCase.label: COLD CASE

finalTwist.title: This Was Never About Riya
finalTwist.description: Threat intelligence confirms SilverKey operates at scale — harvesting OAuth tokens across dozens of SaaS platforms simultaneously. Riya was not targeted personally. She was simply in the wrong place at the right time for a broker running a mass-phishing operation. The winning bid of $800 came from a wallet linked to an industrial espionage group targeting Series B and C tech startups. The downloaded roadmaps were sent to a competitor. The buyer's full identity requires a deeper dark web investigation. Nikhil's SSO misconfiguration — leaving refresh tokens non-expiring — is what turned a 1-hour token theft into a 6-week compromise.
finalTwist.conclusion:
✔ Session hijacking confirmed — no password needed
✔ Phishing page captured OAuth refresh token at SSO redirect step 6 weeks ago
✔ Token never revoked — SSO misconfiguration (Nikhil) enabled indefinite reuse
✔ Simultaneous sessions from Mumbai and Frankfurt — physically impossible
✔ Browser fingerprint confirmed different physical hardware — not a VPN
✔ Frankfurt IP linked to dark web broker SilverKey — 14 prior token sales
✔ Access sold for $800 — buyer linked to industrial espionage group
⚠ Buyer's full identity requires further dark web investigation
